items.q: What does the M+ peak in a mass spectrum represent?
items.correct: The molecular ion — formed when a single electron is removed from the whole molecule, giving the relative molecular mass.
items.misconception: The M+ peak shows the mass of the largest fragment after the molecule breaks apart.
items.random_wrong: The M+ peak shows the mass of a single hydrogen atom added to the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse the M+ peak (the parent ion at the molecular mass) with fragment peaks; they think the highest mass peak is always a fragment, not the intact ionised molecule.

items.q: Can a single standard electrode potential be measured in isolation?
items.correct: No — electrode potentials are RELATIVE values measured against a reference (the standard hydrogen electrode, defined as 0 V).
items.misconception: Each electrode has an absolute potential that can be measured directly with a voltmeter.
items.random_wrong: Standard electrode potentials are measured by submerging the electrode in pure water.
items.why: Students think a voltmeter can give a single electrode's value; they don't grasp that potential difference requires two electrodes by definition.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: In mass spec, ions are sorted by:
items.why: It's m/z ratio. “Charge alone” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Convergence in emission spectra corresponds to:
items.why: It's Ionisation. “Bond formation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If an emission line has frequency 4.57 × 10¹⁴ Hz, what is its wavelength in nm? (c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m/s)
items.why: The real figure is 656. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
